project_overview.project_name: Guinea Kounki Fisheries and Aquaculture Project
project_overview.project_development_objective: Strengthen climate resilience and improve value chain development in fishing and aquaculture communities in target areas
project_overview.total_financing: US$128.0 million (IDA: US$100.0 million, AFD: US$23.0 million, PROBLUE: US$5.0 million)
project_overview.beneficiaries: Approximately 65,000 people including small-scale fishers, fish farmers, fishmongers, processors, traders, women (40% of fishing workforce), youth, and public institution staff. Direct beneficiaries in 10 targeted coastal and rural areas (5 coastal fishing sites, 1 continental fishing site, 4 aquaculture development areas)

geographic_scope.coverage_type: National with focus on 10 targeted areas (5 coastal, 1 continental, 4 rural aquaculture)
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: Partial - Guinea is governed by transitional military government since September 2021 coup; political stability uncertain but government has reaffirmed commitment to sector through national development strategy (Simandou 2040)

key_components.component_name: C1. Sustainable, climate resilient and community-led fisheries management
key_components.description: Strengthen fisheries governance through science-based management plans, community-led initiatives, MCS systems, transparency initiatives, and institutional capacity building. Support research, innovation and conservation including marine protected area management, scientific research programs, climate-smart innovations, marine plastic pollution prevention, and climate change monitoring.
key_components.financing: US$31.0 million (IDA: US$24.0M, AFD: US$4.5M, PROBLUE: US$2.5M)

key_components.description: Improve small-scale fisheries value chains through climate-resilient infrastructure (5 coastal and 1 inland landing sites), sanitary improvements, maritime professional training. Develop aquaculture through capacity building, 4 aquaculture hubs with hatcheries and feed production facilities, and sustainable local feed production.
key_components.financing: US$76.0 million (IDA: US$57.0M, AFD: US$18.0M, PROBLUE: US$1.0M)

key_components.component_name: C3. Resilience and adaptive capacity of targeted communities
key_components.description: Establish partial portfolio credit guarantee (PPCG) mechanism for aquaculture and fisheries MSMEs through FGPE (US$5M endowment). Support social investments including livelihood diversification (honey production, sustainable fuelwood, plastic recycling, composting, market gardening, ecosystem restoration, feed ingredient production).
key_components.financing: US$15.0 million (IDA: US$13.0M, AFD: US$0.5M, PROBLUE: US$1.5M)

key_components.description: Support project coordination, oversight, fiduciary management, environmental and social compliance, monitoring and evaluation, and grievance redress mechanisms.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Fisheries and Maritime Economy (MPEM) through dedicated Project Implementation Team (PIT) to be established within 2 months of financing agreement signature
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Steering Committee (PSC) chaired by MPEM Minister (semi-annual meetings); Project Technical Committee (PTC) chaired by MPEM General Secretariat (monthly meetings); Memoranda of Understanding with implementing partners (ANAG, CNSHB, CNSP, ONSPA, OGPNRF, FGPE, CFPM, ANAFIC, AGEE)
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (February 2025); use of STEP online platform; prior and post review procedures; major procurements include construction of landing sites, aquaculture hubs, office rehabilitation, equipment and vehicles; overall procurement risk rated High with mitigation through staffing, capacity building, and close supervision
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: MPEM/PIT responsible for M&E; M&E Specialist to oversee Results Framework monitoring; quarterly Interim Financial Reports; annual audited financial statements; half-yearly and annual progress reports to World Bank; mid-term and final evaluations; use of GEMS (Geo-Enabling initiative for Monitoring and Supervision); World Bank implementation support missions at least every 6 months

identified_risks.risk_description: Guinea governed by transitional military government since September 2021 coup; unclear timeline for return to civilian rule; potential instability during transition
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Strengthen policy dialogue under World Bank Country Management Unit leadership; solid institutional arrangements for collaboration and autonomy; government reaffirmed commitment through Simandou 2040 strategy and national artisanal fisheries program

identified_risks.risk_description: Guinea's economy vulnerable to external shocks (Ebola, commodity price declines); limited economic diversification
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Transition government implementing economic and human resources reforms; stable monetary policy; settlement of payment arrears; public finance consolidation; World Bank monitoring; investments in agriculture, fisheries, aquaculture support economic diversification

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak project implementation capacities across World Bank portfolio; MPEM lacks recent project management experience (WARFP ended 2022); multiple implementing agencies with coordination challenges
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Institutional capacity-building programs; strategic partnerships; clear ownership and responsibilities; dedicated PIT within MPEM; budgeted resources for capacity strengthening at central and decentralized levels; thorough capacity diagnostics; close World Bank supervision

identified_risks.risk_description: Challenging FM and procurement environment; political volatility raises concerns about mismanagement, fraud, lack of transparency and corruption; MPEM/PIT not yet established; FM mechanisms not in place; initial FM risk rated High
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: FM and procurement assessments conducted; action plans with specific mitigation measures; recruitment of FM Specialist, accountants, internal auditor, procurement specialist; finalization of procedures manual; installation of accounting software (Tompro); external audits by independent firms; quarterly IFRs; annual audited financial statements; close World Bank supervision; 4 ISM in first 18 months

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial risks including: biodiversity loss from fishing/aquaculture practices; water pollution from aquaculture; civil engineering works risks (poor working conditions, child labor, SEA/SH, exclusion of vulnerable groups); physical and economic resettlement; restriction of access to marine resources; cultural heritage damage; security force abuse; risks to female MSMEs through financial intermediaries
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF) and Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared and disclosed; Process Framework for access restrictions; detailed ESIAs/ESMPs when locations confirmed; Labor Management Procedure; recruitment of Senior Environmental Specialist, Senior Social Development Specialist, Gender Specialist; capacity building on ESF; collaboration with OGPNRF for protected area management; Institutional Assessment of OGPNRF; waste management measures; FGPE ESMS updated for fisheries window; E&S Commitment Plan developed

identified_risks.risk_description: New intervention areas require careful implementation; some sites (aquaculture hubs, inland fishing) depend on ongoing feasibility studies; information systems and new technologies require troubleshooting
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Preparatory studies launched to inform major investments; bidding documents to be ready soon after effectiveness; special attention to new areas during implementation; rapid issue identification and troubleshooting; continued civil society engagement; regular communication on natural resource management decisions

identified_risks.risk_description: Need to strengthen fisheries management and reform to meet community needs and address IUU fishing; limited knowledge on continental fishing; capacity constraints for MCS and scientific advice
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project supports development of science-based fisheries management plans; strengthens MCS system; builds institutional capacity; supports research programs; promotes community-led management; develops sustainable financing mechanisms for fisheries management services

identified_risks.risk_description: Need for broad consensus on fisheries management reforms; diverse stakeholders including informal fishers; deeply ingrained traditional practices; vulnerable populations dependent on fisheries
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Stakeholder Engagement Plan (SEP) finalized and disclosed; active community involvement in all project phases; community-led fisheries management with technical assistance; grievance redress mechanism through ANAFIC; periodic consultations and satisfaction surveys; women's representation quotas in governance bodies

triggered_safeguard_policies: ESS1: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts
ESS3: Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management
ESS5: Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement
ESS6: Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources
ESS10: Stakeholder Engagement and Information Disclosure
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: Yes - Project-level grievance mechanism through existing national GM managed by ANAFIC with grievance management committees in all municipalities; MoU to be signed with ANAFIC for adequate GM oversight; grievances addressed within project-approved timeline (target 100%); World Bank Grievance Redress Service (GRS) and Accountability Mechanism also available
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Significant focus on gender equality: women constitute 40% of fishing workforce but face inequalities in finance access, technical resources, job opportunities; project targets women-owned MSMEs with dedicated technical assistance; establishes childcare facilities at landing sites; promotes women's leadership through quotas in community-led fisheries management (at least 1 woman in executive positions); supports women's capacity building in treasury management, entrepreneurship, literacy; intermediate indicators track women entrepreneurs accessing funding and women in decision-making roles; project aligned with Guinea's National Gender Policy and World Bank Gender Strategy 2024-2030
vulnerable_groups: Women (40% of fishing workforce, facing gender inequalities)
Coastal and rural communities dependent on fisheries
Communities in marine protected areas with access restrictions
Communities vulnerable to climate change impacts

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project addresses conflict risks through: (1) Community-led fisheries management promoting rural cohesion and resilience of coastal populations; (2) Strengthening governance and transparency to reduce IUU fishing and associated conflicts; (3) Inclusive stakeholder engagement with diverse actors; (4) Support for alternative livelihoods reducing pressure on fisheries; (5) Grievance redress mechanisms for conflict resolution; (6) Women's empowerment and leadership roles; (7) Institutional capacity building for effective management; (8) Focus on vulnerable populations and food security
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Guinea faces multiple fragility factors: (1) Political fragility - transitional military government since 2021 coup with uncertain timeline for civilian rule; (2) Economic fragility - vulnerable to external shocks (Ebola, commodity prices), limited diversification, high poverty and food insecurity (21% of households food insecure in 2023); (3) Environmental fragility - climate change vulnerability (ranked 161st of 185 countries), deforestation, mangrove degradation, coastal erosion; (4) Institutional fragility - weak implementation capacity, limited FM and procurement capabilities, corruption concerns; (5) Social fragility - high malnutrition (24% chronic malnutrition in children), gender inequality (ranked 157th in Gender Inequality Index), limited access to services; (6) Sectoral fragility - fish stock degradation, IUU fishing (40-65% of catch in Gulf of Guinea), limited fisheries management capacity
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Security considerations include: (1) Risks from security forces used as patrols in marine protected areas - Institutional Assessment to evaluate capacity for community outreach, law enforcement, incident management, collaboration with other agencies; (2) Risks of abuse by eco-guards and enforcement personnel - capacity building and oversight mechanisms; (3) Sea safety risks - project supports geolocation technologies for rescue operations, early warning systems, vessel stability criteria, communication systems for extreme weather; (4) Monitoring, Control and Surveillance (MCS) system strengthening to address IUU fishing and associated security challenges; (5) Coastal security in context of maritime activities
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: Yes - Partial: (1) Physical resettlement risk - project involves construction of landing sites and aquaculture hubs at locations not yet fully confirmed; some sites have been pre-selected but technical studies ongoing; (2) Economic resettlement risk - restrictions on access to marine resources in protected areas will affect some communities; (3) Mitigation measures - Resettlement Policy Framework (RPF) prepared and to be disclosed; Process Framework developed to determine potential restrictions and mitigation measures including alternative livelihoods support; detailed Resettlement Plans (RPs) to be prepared when exact locations known; support for affected communities through livelihood diversification activities; grievance redress mechanisms for affected populations; (4) No refugee-specific issues mentioned but project addresses food security and livelihood resilience for vulnerable populations
